channel.title: MikeCast — Daily Briefing
channel.description: Your daily AI-powered news briefing. Personalized news across AI & Tech, Business & Markets, Companies, and NY Sports.
channel.itunes:author: MikeCast

item.title: MikeCast — March 04, 2026
item.description:
**INTRO**
Hey there, welcome to MikeCast—it’s March 4th, 2026, and I've got a steaming cup of news ready for you. Today, we're diving into some big shifts in the tech world, including a game-changing settlement between Google and Epic. Plus, the latest on AI's energy crunch and more excitement in the crypto market. We'll also check in on some juicy updates from Apple and Amazon. And of course, I’ve got the latest from the New York sports scene. So, let's get into it!
**AI & TECH**
Alright, let’s kick things off with the tech titans. Google and Epic Games have finally settled their long-running antitrust battle over app store fees. This deal marks the end of the era where a 30% cut was the norm. Google’s now dropping its Play Store commissions to 20%, with an option for developers to use their own billing systems. This is huge for app developers everywhere. It means more flexibility, less cost, and ultimately, more innovation in the app space. It’s a win for competition and consumer choice.
Meanwhile, former President Trump has announced a pledge from major AI companies like Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI to cover the costs of power plants and grid upgrades needed for their massive data centers. This comes as AI's energy consumption is under the spotlight. It’s a smart move to address both environmental concerns and infrastructure needs, especially as AI continues to grow at breakneck speed.
Now, a bit of drama in the AI world—Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei has accused OpenAI of lying about a Pentagon deal. Anthropic walked away from a military contract over AI safety concerns, only for OpenAI to step in. This could spark a broader conversation about ethics and transparency in AI, which is something we definitely need more of.
And speaking of AI advancements, Microsoft has just rolled out Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact AI model that's packing a punch. It’s designed to know when to think and when to save resources, which could be a game-changer for efficiency in AI applications.
**BUSINESS & MARKETS**
Switching gears to business and markets—Broadcom is riding the AI wave, reporting a 106% jump in AI revenue. Infrastructure spending is booming, and Broadcom's benefiting big time. This just shows how central AI has become to growth strategies across industries.
And over in the wild world of crypto, Coinbase shares surged 12% after President Trump expressed support for a new digital asset market structure bill. If passed, this bill could open the door for crypto firms to issue yield-bearing stablecoins, potentially bringing more stability and legitimacy to the market. Crypto enthusiasts, keep your eyes on this one.
**COMPANIES**
Now, let’s talk about some cool company news. Amazon's rolled out a new AI tool for sellers, helping them visualize and grow their businesses in real time. It’s all about making data more actionable and giving sellers a competitive edge.
And Apple fans, get excited—the new MacBook Pro with M5 Pro and M5 Max chips is here. But that's not all—Apple also unveiled the MacBook Neo, which is their latest addition to the lineup. With all these options, Apple is clearly aiming to cater to both the power users and the everyday consumer.
**NY SPORTS**
Alright, sports fans, let's hit the field. Yankees star Giancarlo Stanton made a solid spring training debut, looking sharp and ready for the season. And in case you missed it, the Yankees faced off against the Red Sox today—a classic rivalry that never fails to deliver excitement. Finally, there’s some buzz about whether the Yankees should lock in Jazz Chisholm Jr. with a long-term extension. It seems like an interesting move that could bolster their lineup for years to come.
**OUTRO**
And that wraps up today’s episode of MikeCast! Thanks for tuning in. If you enjoyed the show, be sure to subscribe and share it with a friend. I’m Mike, signing off—catch you tomorrow with more news you need to know. Stay curious!
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — March 04, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — March 03, 2026
item.description:
[INTRO]
Hey there, welcome to MikeCast! It's March 3rd, 2026, and I'm your host, Mike, here to get you up to speed on today's big stories. We’ve got a lot on our plate today, from the high-stakes battle over electric vehicles to some geopolitical shake-ups in the Middle East. Plus, we’ll dive into the latest on the markets and catch you up on some company news that’s making waves. And, of course, we’ll wrap it up with a quick sports update. So grab your coffee, and let's dive right in!
[AI & TECH SEGMENT]
Alright, let's kick things off with the automotive world, where big names like General Motors and Ford are at a crossroads. The dilemma? Whether to double down on electric vehicles and self-driving tech or stick with traditional gas cars. The pressure's on as Chinese carmakers and tech giants race ahead in innovation. It's a make-or-break moment, really. If these American icons don’t adapt quickly, they risk being left in the dust — or worse, becoming obsolete. This isn't just about cars; it’s about the future of transportation and who controls it. It’s exciting and nerve-wracking all at once.
Switching gears to a more somber note, the situation in the Middle East is heating up. Israel, with support from the U.S., is pressing hard against Iran, which is leading to a reconfiguration of alliances and power dynamics in the region. For Israel, it's a moment of opportunity to strengthen its position, but for the world, it’s a precarious situation that could have wide-reaching implications.
And speaking of implications, global markets are reacting — and not in a good way. Stocks and bonds are taking a hit as investors scramble to assess the fallout from the conflict. It’s a reminder of how interconnected our economies are. What happens in Tehran doesn’t stay in Tehran; it ripples across the globe, touching everything from energy prices to investor confidence.
[BUSINESS & MARKETS SEGMENT]
Now, let's talk money. The conflict in the Middle East is more than just a geopolitical issue; it’s a potential economic shockwave. We’re already seeing energy prices surge, which could mean broader inflation on the horizon. For businesses and consumers alike, this could translate to higher costs and tighter budgets. It’s a tough pill to swallow, but being prepared is half the battle.
On a lighter note, tariffs are once again causing headaches for small businesses. Chocolate makers and fig-paste importers are tangled up in confusion over whether to seek refunds for tariffs overturned by the Supreme Court. It’s a classic case of bureaucracy versus business, and it highlights the ongoing challenges that small companies face in navigating trade policies.
[COMPANIES SEGMENT]
Alright, let’s shift to the corporate world. Apple just launched the new iPad Air with the M4 chip, alongside the iPhone 17e. Apple continues to push the envelope with its tech offerings, and these releases are sure to keep the competition on their toes. If you're an Apple fan, it’s a good time to be alive!
Meanwhile, Amazon’s facing some turbulence in the Middle East. Drones have damaged three of their facilities in the UAE and Bahrain, and a fire at one of their data centers isn’t helping matters. It’s a stark reminder of the vulnerabilities that even the biggest companies have in volatile regions.
And in tech partnerships, NVIDIA and Coherent are teaming up to develop optics technology for data centers. This could be a game-changer for how data is managed and scaled in the future. It’s all about speed and efficiency, and this partnership is set to push the boundaries.
[NY SPORTS SEGMENT]
Alright, New York sports fans, here’s your quick rundown. The Yankees edged out the Phillies with a 5-3 win, and veteran outfielder Randal Grichuk has been invited to spring training. Could he be the secret weapon this season? Time will tell.
Over in basketball, the Knicks sent a clear message with their dominating win over the Spurs. It’s anyone's guess what this unpredictable...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — March 03, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — March 02, 2026
item.description:
Hey there, it's Mike and welcome to another episode of MikeCast for March 2nd, 2026. Grab your coffee, get comfy, and let's dive into today's top stories. We've got a colorful character making waves in the Pentagon, a big shift in the U.S. military stance, and some tech giants making deals that could impact us all. Plus, we'll check in on the markets, and of course, catch up with some juicy sports updates. Alright, let's get started.
First up in AI & Tech, we've got Palmer Luckey, the Hawaiian shirt-wearing founder of Anduril, turning heads at the Pentagon. Luckey's company is at the forefront of autonomous weaponry, and with President Trump pushing to modernize the military, Luckey's become the go-to guy. Now, why does this matter? Well, the integration of AI in military applications is a game-changer. It could redefine how conflicts are conducted and shift the power balance in global military tech.
Speaking of AI, OpenAI has amended its deal with the Pentagon to include new protections against using its tech for mass surveillance of Americans. This is a big one. As AI becomes more ingrained in defense strategies, ensuring the right ethical boundaries are in place is crucial to maintaining public trust and protecting civil liberties.
In a related vein, the Trump administration's military strategy is shifting. Post-9/11 caution seems to be fading as President Trump embraces military power more aggressively, notably with recent actions in Iran. This new approach suggests a move away from the war-wary stance of the past two decades. This could have far-reaching consequences for global stability and U.S. foreign relations.
Alright, switching gears to Business & Markets. Rising tensions in the Middle East have sent oil prices soaring, which means we could see a spike at the gas pump just as the busy driving season kicks in. If you're planning a summer road trip, you might want to budget a bit more for fuel.
And over in Dubai, travelers are in a bind as Iran targets the region in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks. Dubai's usually a safe haven, but these recent events have thrown a wrench in its reputation. It's a reminder of how quickly geopolitical tensions can disrupt even the most stable environments.
Now, let's talk companies. Apple's making waves with the iPhone 17e and a new iPad Air featuring the M4 chip. It's always exciting to see where Apple is pushing the envelope with its tech, but it also makes you wonder — how are we going to keep up with all these upgrades?
Meanwhile, a Super PAC owned by Meta is spending big in the Texas primaries. It's a reminder of how much money is pumped into politics, and how tech giants increasingly influence the political landscape.
And speaking of Meta, there's been a buzz about a new Facebook policy that supposedly lets them do whatever they want with your photos. Yikes! Always a good idea to check those privacy settings and stay informed about what you're sharing and with whom.
Alright, time for some NY Sports. The Yankees' 2026 Top 30 prospects list is out, and there are some promising names to watch. Plus, they've invited veteran outfielder Randal Grichuk to spring training. It's a move that could add some seasoned depth to their outfield lineup.
And for those following MLB trade rumors, the Yankees are eyeing a $4.2 million shortstop from the Nationals. There's always something to keep an eye on with the Yankees—never a dull moment in the Bronx.
Before we wrap up, let's dive into Big Mike's Picks. Today, I'm recommending you check out the New York Times article on how Trump decided to go to war. It's a deep dive into the decision-making process and the influence of international allies. Definitely worth a read if you want to understand the complexities behind military decisions.
Alright, that's a wrap for today's episode of MikeCast. Thanks for tuning in and catching up with me. Don't forget to subscribe, give us a review if you like what you hear, and tell your friend...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — March 02, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — March 01, 2026
item.description:
**INTRO**
Hey there, welcome back to MikeCast on this beautiful March 1st, 2026! I'm Mike, your trusty guide through today's whirlwind of news. So, what's brewing today? We're diving into the unexpected comeback of an old-school tech favorite—the iPod. Plus, a shake-up in the AI world as OpenAI gets cozy with the Pentagon. And in a dramatic international turn, big changes in Iran's leadership are making waves. Stick around; we've got lots to unpack today!
**AI & TECH**
Let's kick things off with a blast from the past—the iPod. Yep, you heard that right. The iconic music device from Apple, once thought to be gathering dust on a shelf, is making a comeback. Young music listeners are picking it up as a way to disconnect a bit from the constant buzz of streaming services. It's fascinating, right? In a world that's always online, people are longing for a little digital detox, and the iPod is their ticket. It's not exactly vinyl, but it's a step back to a simpler era of music listening.
Now, over to the AI scene, where things have been nothing short of a rollercoaster. The Pentagon has decided to partner with OpenAI, dropping Anthropic from their roster. This is huge. OpenAI's partnership could signal a new era of AI integration in military operations. It raises questions about how AI could reshape defense strategies and what that means for international security. Definitely something to keep an eye on.
And let's not overlook the geopolitical tremors following a major intelligence operation. The U.S. and Israel's collaboration led to the killing of Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. This isn't just a headline; it's a seismic shift in Middle Eastern politics. Khamenei was a pivotal figure, and his death could alter the regional power dynamics significantly. It's a story with layers, touching on diplomacy, military strategy, and the future of Iran.
**BUSINESS & MARKETS**
Switching gears to business, let's talk about the Persian Gulf's oil situation. Following the recent attack in Iran, tankers are avoiding the region, which could spike oil prices. This affects everything from your gas bill to global markets. It's a reminder of how interconnected our economies are and how quickly global events can hit our wallets.
And in the corporate world, it's the end of an era at Berkshire Hathaway. Warren Buffett's stepping down, and Gregory Abel has taken the helm. His first report showed a drop in earnings, especially in the insurance sector. Abel's got big shoes to fill, and it'll be interesting to see how he navigates the legacy left by Buffett. A new chapter for Berkshire, for sure.
**COMPANIES**
Alright, now onto some company news. Apple’s in the spotlight again, with their devices now approved to handle classified NATO information. That's a big thumbs up for Apple’s security protocols and a nod to their growing influence in not just consumer tech, but now international security.
Meanwhile, Meta has launched legal action against scam advertisers. With the rise of digital scams, it's good to see a tech giant taking a stand. Hopefully, this will lead to a cleaner, safer online space for all of us.
Lastly, if you're feeling overwhelmed by Big Tech, The Guardian's got you covered with a guide on how to ditch the giants like Amazon and Google. It's part of a growing movement to regain control over our digital lives. Food for thought if you're considering a tech cleanse.
**NY SPORTS**
Zooming into the sports world, Yankees fans, listen up! Brian Cashman is optimistic about the team's young arms and the eventual return of Gerrit Cole. They're gearing up for what could be an exciting season. On the court, the Knicks are set to face the San Antonio Spurs. Should be a good matchup. If you're into basketball, don't miss it!
**MIKE'S PICKS**
And now, for Big Mike’s hand-picked reads of the day. I've got my eye on an intriguing piece about how Kickstarter is thriving under Everette Taylor's leadership. It's all about runnin...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — March 01, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 28, 2026
item.description:
Hey there, welcome to MikeCast! It's February 28, 2026, and I'm here to catch you up on today's need-to-know stories. We've got a packed lineup today, from high-stakes AI showdowns to dramatic moves in the business world. Plus, we’ll check in with the latest on the Yankees. Let's dive right in!
First up, the tech world is buzzing about Changpeng Zhao, the founder of Binance. In a draft of his memoir, CZ spills the beans on secret talks that landed him in prison and his run-in with ICE. This tale of crypto fame turned cautionary is a wild ride. If anything, it highlights how volatile the world of cryptocurrency can be. Even the biggest fish aren't immune to turbulent waters.
Now, onto a big tech clash — Silicon Valley is rallying behind Anthropic, an AI startup, after President Trump and the Pentagon’s actions created friction. This is more than just a tech industry squabble. It’s a pivotal moment for AI’s role in national security. Who gets to decide the safeguards on these technologies? It's a reminder of how these tools are shaping our world and the power struggle that comes with it.
Speaking of AI and the Pentagon, OpenAI has cut a deal with the Defense Department, just hours after Trump ordered federal agencies to stop using Anthropic's tech. It’s a classic tale of one company’s loss being another’s gain. This agreement could set a precedent for future partnerships between tech firms and the military.
And a quick note on Instagram — they're rolling out a feature to alert parents if their teens search for self-harm content. It's an opt-in feature, but it could be a crucial tool for parents trying to keep their kids safe online. It's good to see tech companies stepping up for user safety.
Alright, switching gears to business! Netflix is cutting ties with Warner Bros. Discovery. Some analysts are saying this split might actually benefit Netflix. It's a chance for them to refocus on their core strengths without the massive burden of an $83 billion deal. Meanwhile, Warner Bros. employees are facing uncertainty with Paramount stepping in. Big changes are afoot, and it could reshape the entertainment landscape.
On a lighter note, if you’re planning for retirement, there are some smart strategies to make your savings last. It’s all about balance — enjoying your nest egg without burning through it too quickly. Definitely worth a read if you're nearing those golden years.
Now, let’s talk companies. Apple’s seen a drop in value today, and everyone’s curious why. It's a reminder of how sensitive markets can be to speculation and rumor.
Meta is taking legal action against scam advertisers. It's about time, right? With the digital world getting trickier to navigate, this move could help clean up the ad space.
Meanwhile, we’ve got an exciting new partnership between OpenAI and Amazon. These two giants working together could mean big things for AI development and e-commerce. The possibilities are endless!
Alright, let's hit the sports scene. The Yankees are making waves with Randal Grichuk getting a shot at the roster. It’s always exciting when fresh talent gets a chance to shine. Plus, they absolutely crushed the Twins in Fort Myers — what a game!
And for the basketball fans, a former Knicks center is lighting it up with a conference rival. It’s always fun to see players find their groove, even if it’s not with the home team.
Alright, before we wrap up, let's check out Big Mike's hand-picked reads. If you’re into engineering and craftsmanship, the British watch brand Apiar is doing some cool stuff with 3D printing. They’re stretching beyond traditional watchmaking, and it’s fascinating to see tech and tradition collide.
That’s a wrap for today’s MikeCast! Thanks for hanging out with me. Don't forget to hit subscribe so you never miss an episode. And if you enjoyed today’s show, share it with a friend. I'm Mike, signing off. Catch you tomorrow!
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 28, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 27, 2026
item.description:
Hey there, welcome back to MikeCast! It’s February 27, 2026, and I’m your host, Mike. Today we’ve got some hot stories from the world of tech, business shake-ups, and a wild ride in sports. Plus, a couple of my top picks you won't want to miss. So grab your coffee, settle in, and let’s dive right in.
Starting with AI and tech, we have an intriguing story from Europe. The founders of HateAid, a German human-rights group that’s been helping women fight online abuse, have been barred from entering the U.S. The Trump administration accuses them of being part of a so-called “global censorship-industrial complex.” Now, this isn’t just a bureaucratic squabble; it raises big questions about how nations balance free speech with protecting individuals online. Are we heading towards a clash of values between countries? It’s a complex issue that’ll keep evolving.
Meanwhile, in Elon Musk’s world, he’s back with another bold proclamation: a future of “sustainable abundance.” Musk envisions a society where robots cater to all our needs, theoretically freeing humans from the grind of work. It sounds utopian, right? But it also raises questions about the economic and social structures that would need to change to make this dream a reality. As we’ve seen, automation has a way of shaking things up — just look at India, where AI is starting to shrink the tech industry’s traditional stronghold on white-collar jobs. Adaptation is key here, and it'll be fascinating to watch how India navigates this challenge.
And speaking of Musk, the man’s got a secret web of companies in Texas — about 90 of them! He’s using these for everything from paying nannies to buying land and even supporting Donald Trump’s re-election. This sprawling network gives us a peek into how billionaires like Musk are not just entrepreneurs but also influential political players. It’s a reminder of how interconnected business and politics can be.
Alright, switching gears to business and markets. Big news for CNN and its future — Paramount seems to have edged out Netflix in securing Warner Bros. Discovery. This potential acquisition could shift the media landscape significantly, especially for CNN, which might face some uncertain times ahead. With the Ellison family on the brink of building a colossal media empire, we might see a new era of influence in how news and entertainment are delivered. It’s something to keep an eye on if you’re into media dynamics.
In other news, the rise and fall of a 3-D printing empire. Desktop Metal, once a billion-dollar start-up, has filed for bankruptcy. It promised to revolutionize manufacturing but couldn’t quite deliver. The 3-D printing industry seems to be taking a more sober turn, and it’s a cautionary tale about the hype versus the reality of tech innovation.
Now let’s talk companies. Over at Meta, things are heating up. They’re taking legal action against scam advertisers, which is a step in the right direction for protecting users. But there’s also some internal drama — a Meta executive warned that the plan to encrypt Facebook Messenger was “so irresponsible.” It’s a classic debate between privacy and security, and how Meta handles this could set a precedent for other tech giants.
And over at Amazon, they’re noting some interesting shopping trends. Everyone seems to be buying a $9 kitchen hack and a K-Beauty mask that’s sold over 100,000 times. It’s fascinating how certain products capture the public’s imagination and become viral hits.
Alright, time for a quick sports update. Giancarlo Stanton of the Yankees is determined to play through pain, despite saying he “can’t open a bottle.” Ouch, right? It’s always impressive to see athletes push through, but it does raise concerns about player health and longevity. Will Stanton be able to last the whole season? Fans are definitely hoping he can.
Finally, Big Mike's hand-picked reads for today. If you’re curious about how Pakistan and Afghanistan are clashing or want to dive into South ...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 27, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 26, 2026
item.description:
Hey there, it’s February 26, 2026, and you’re listening to MikeCast — your daily serving of news with a side of insight. I'm Mike, catching you up on what’s buzzing today. We’ve got AI raking in billions for Nvidia, privacy concerns with chatbots, and a possible military strike in Iran that’s stirring the geopolitical pot. Plus, how MrBeast’s team is tangled in insider trading. And let’s not forget, Jalen Brunson’s big-time aspirations with the Knicks. Alright, let's dive right in!
Kicking things off in the AI & Tech world, Nvidia has hit a massive milestone — $43 billion in quarterly profit thanks to their AI chip sales. Just three years ago, their yearly profit was $4.4 billion. Talk about a glow-up! AI is clearly the golden goose right now, driving Nvidia’s skyrocketing success. So, what does this mean for us? Well, it highlights how AI is becoming an integral part of our lives and economy. It’s reshaping industries and, as we see here, creating major economic shifts.
Speaking of AI, let’s chat about privacy — or the lack thereof. As AI gets more woven into everyday life, privacy risks are evolving too. People are sharing sensitive data with chatbots without a second thought. But here’s the kicker: if those chats reveal plans for violence, is there a duty to warn the authorities? This question isn’t just academic; it’s the crux of a growing debate about privacy versus safety in the digital age.
And if you’re a parent, listen up: AI-generated videos are flooding YouTube, and they’re not as harmless as they appear. Experts warn these videos can be cognitively overwhelming for kids, lacking proper structure and often spreading conflicting information. It’s a digital jungle out there, so keeping an eye on what your kiddos are watching is more important than ever.
Alright, switching gears to Business & Markets. There's a buzz in NYC — our new mayor, Zohran Mamdani, needs a nickname. The city’s tabloids are having a field day with this, from “Z” to “Zo.” But let's talk economic impact: a new mayor means new policies, which could shift the local business landscape. We’ll just have to wait and see how this plays out.
And in a bizarre twist, a MrBeast employee is under fire for alleged insider trading. The prediction-market platform, Kalshi, has already reported this to regulators. This one’s a reminder that even in the wild west of online markets, rules still apply. And speaking of rules, there’s a pushback against plans to mint gold coins featuring President Trump for America’s 250th birthday. It’s not just about the bling — it’s a clash over legacy and symbolism.
Now, onto companies! Apple stock is on the rise again — always a crowd favorite in the market. Meanwhile, Meta and AMD are teaming up for a long-term AI infrastructure pact. This is big. It shows how tech giants are pooling resources to stay ahead in the AI race. Partnerships like this could set the stage for the next wave of technological advancements.
And let’s not forget about Mary Craven, who passed away at 79. Known for guiding North Syracuse’s ‘Apple Muffin Gang’ back in the day, she left quite an impression. A little nostalgia to remind us of the impact one person can have on their community.
Alright, sports fans, here’s your quick NY sports rundown. The Yankees are gearing up to honor CC Sabathia with a jersey retirement and plaque ceremony in September. A true legend! And in Knicks news, Jalen Brunson’s quest for a championship ring could solidify his status as a team icon. The anticipation is real, folks.
Finally, in Big Mike's hand-picked reads, I'm recommending you check out the piece on the missing Epstein files and the gun battle off the coast of Cuba. It’s a mix of mystery and real-world intrigue that’s worth exploring.
Alright, that’s all for today. Thanks for tuning in to MikeCast! If you liked what you heard, don’t forget to subscribe and share with your friends. Catch you tomorrow for another round of news and insights. Until then, stay cu...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 26, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 25, 2026
item.description:
[INTRO] Hey there, everyone! Welcome back to MikeCast, your daily dive into the headlines with a splash of insight and a whole lot of energy. It's February 25, 2026, and today we've got a lot on our plate. From Netflix's bold new moves in the podcast world to big news in the AI and tech space, plus some intriguing developments in the business world. And, of course, we can't forget the latest buzz in New York sports. So grab your coffee, settle in, and let's get started.
[AI & TECH] Alright, first up, let's dive into the fascinating world of AI and tech. Netflix is making waves again, this time with its debut podcast lineup, featuring shows like "The Pete Davidson Show." This move is stirring up questions about what really defines a podcast. Is it audio content? Video? Both? Netflix seems to be betting on the hybrid model, hoping to capture the audience's attention across multiple formats. If you're a content creator or simply a media enthusiast, this shift could signal a whole new way of consuming media.
Speaking of AI, there’s been a lot of buzz about tools designed to spot AI-generated fakes. While these detectors are getting more sophisticated, they aren't foolproof. After running over 1,000 tests, researchers found strengths but also plenty of weaknesses. So, the next time you see a suspiciously perfect photo or video, you might wonder if it's the real deal.
And over in Europe, Wayve, a driverless car start-up, just raised a whopping $1.2 billion. They're using AI to take autonomous driving to the next level. It's a big step forward for the tech, and it's going to be interesting to see how this plays out on the roads, especially with safety concerns and regulations still very much in the air.
Lastly, the Pentagon's putting pressure on AI company Anthropic. They’ve given the company an ultimatum regarding how their tech is used. Anthropic wants to set limits, while the Pentagon's looking at potential risks in the supply chain. It’s a classic case of innovation clashing with security concerns, and how this unfolds could set precedents for tech companies working with the government.
[BUSINESS & MARKETS] Switching gears to business and markets, there’s a bit of a stir on Wall Street over a research report from Citrini Research. This report paints a pretty grim picture of job losses and stock market sell-offs due to AI. Not everyone agrees with the findings, though. It's got analysts and economists debating the real impact of AI on the economy. If you’re investing or just watching the market, it’s a story to keep an eye on.
Meanwhile, Abu Dhabi’s state oil company is making a strategic pivot. They're looking beyond oil, with plans to expand into natural gas, chemicals, and renewables. This is a big move, reflecting a global trend as energy companies diversify in response to climate change and fluctuating oil prices. It’s a classic case of adaptation in a shifting marketplace.
And in the media world, Paramount has upped its bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, offering $31 a share. This could lead to some major shake-ups in the entertainment industry, especially as they compete with giants like Netflix. It’s all about content supremacy, and these mergers could redefine what we watch and how we watch it.
[COMPANIES] Now, let's talk companies. Apple is rolling out new age assurance tools as part of its App Store requirements in several regions. It's all about improving safety and compliance, especially for younger users. These changes could set new standards across the industry.
Meta, on the other hand, is making headlines with its massive $100 billion deal with AMD. They're looking to boost their AI infrastructure, despite some skepticism about an AI bubble. Partnerships like these underline the growing importance of hardware in powering AI advancements.
And in a bit of controversy, a Meta executive has raised concerns about Facebook Messenger's encryption plans, calling them 'irresponsible.' This court filing adds anothe...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 25, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 24, 2026
item.description:
Hey there, welcome back to MikeCast! It's February 24th, 2026, and I'm your host, Mike, here to keep you in the loop with everything going down today. Grab your coffee, and let's get into it. We’ve got some spicy tech intrigue involving Jeffrey Epstein and Microsoft, a look at travel safety in Mexico amidst some unrest, and big moves from OpenAI in the consulting world. And of course, we'll touch on how IBM's feeling the heat from Anthropic. Plus, we’ll check in on the Trump administration’s latest shake-up in unemployment benefits and how Apple’s boosting U.S. manufacturing. And for sports fans, there’s some excitement in the air for Yankees and Knicks followers. So, let's dive right in!
Starting with AI & Tech, the New York Times is out with a jaw-dropping report about Jeffrey Epstein and Microsoft. Turns out, Epstein had his hooks deep into the tech giant for over two decades. He was connected enough to be privy to some pretty high-level chats about company succession and other business matters. Now, this raises all sorts of questions about the ethical oversight in big tech firms and how someone with Epstein's background managed to establish such a network. It’s a reminder that even in tech, where we often think it's just about innovation and data, it’s also deeply about who you know and how you navigate those circles.
Switching gears, if you’re planning a trip south of the border, you might wanna listen up. There’s some unrest in Mexico following the killing of a cartel leader, which has tourists rethinking their plans. According to the New York Times, this wave of violence is affecting popular tourist spots, which could impact your spring break plans. It's a good time to be extra cautious and perhaps reconsider travel dates or destinations.
And in some big news from the world of AI, CNBC reports that OpenAI has inked multiyear deals with some major consulting firms. This is part of their big push into the enterprise market. This move is significant as it shows OpenAI's strategy to become a staple in business operations, leveraging AI to streamline processes and innovate solutions. It’s clear that AI isn’t just a buzzword anymore—it’s becoming an integral part of business infrastructure.
Meanwhile, IBM's having a tough day. According to Yahoo Finance, they’re seeing their biggest stock drop since 2000, thanks to some groundbreaking news from Anthropic. Their new COBOL tool is gaining traction, and it’s putting some serious pressure on IBM. This is a classic case of how innovation in tech can ripple through the industry, shaking up even the giants.
Alright, now let’s talk business and markets. The Trump administration is on a mission to 'reimagine' jobless benefits, especially focusing on states like California. This is part of an antifraud campaign, but it’s also a politically charged move. For those in states led by Democrats, this heightened scrutiny might mean more red tape and delays in receiving benefits. This story is a reminder of how deeply politics can affect everyday economic realities.
In other business news, Centauri Therapeutics is getting a big boost with a £30M extension to their Series A funding. BioSpace reports that this includes a £6M investment from the AMR Action Fund. This is a major step forward for their lead clinical candidate, signaling more progress in therapeutic solutions. If you’re into biotech, this is a company to watch.
Now, onto companies. Apple’s got some exciting news—they’re ramping up U.S. manufacturing, with Mac mini production set for later this year. This move is part of a broader trend of tech companies bringing production stateside, which is great news for job creation and supply chain resilience.
And speaking of Apple, a quick nod to Steve Jobs, who would’ve turned 71 today. His legacy continues to shape tech as we know it, from the devices we use to the way companies think about innovation.
Over at Meta, things are a bit tense. Reuters has a report revealing that a M...
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 24, 2026

item.title: MikeCast — February 23, 2026
item.description:
**INTRO**
Hey there, it’s Mike and welcome to another episode of MikeCast, your go-to spot for all things news, served with a side of insight and a dash of humor. It's February 23, 2026, and we've got a lot to dive into today. We'll be talking about a shake-up in the AI world involving some international intrigue, a rough start to the week on Wall Street, Tesla's latest legal hurdles, and the buzz around Jack Hughes' return to the ice. So grab your coffee, settle in, and let's get into it.
**AI & TECH SEGMENT**
Alright, kicking things off in the tech world today, we’ve got some drama with a capital D. Anthropic, the company behind the AI model Claude, is accusing several Chinese AI companies of "distilling" its technology. Now, if you’re wondering what in the world "distilling" means here, it’s basically a fancy way of saying these companies allegedly siphoned data from Claude to boost their own models. It’s like sneaking a taste of your neighbor’s secret sauce and then claiming your own BBQ prowess.
This isn’t just a tech squabble; it’s a peek into the ongoing global AI arms race. With AI technology shaping everything from our social media feeds to self-driving cars, staying ahead is crucial. The real question is, how do we balance innovation with ethics? And what does this mean for users like you and me? It’s a space to watch, especially as more companies try to safeguard their tech assets.
**BUSINESS & MARKETS SEGMENT**
Now let's talk money. The Dow took quite a tumble, dropping 821 points to kick off Nvidia week. Why Nvidia week, you ask? Well, Nvidia's quarterly earnings report is on the horizon, and the market is feeling jittery. Tech stocks often set the mood on Wall Street, and right now, it’s not exactly a party vibe.
But here's a bit of calm in the storm: a top Fed official mentioned that the Supreme Court's recent tariff ruling isn't expected to shake up the interest rate outlook. This is actually good news for anyone worried about sudden spikes in borrowing costs. So, while the markets might be wobbling, at least we’ve got a steady hand on the monetary policy tiller.
**COMPANIES SEGMENT**
Switching gears to company news, Tesla’s in a bit of a pickle. They’re having some serious issues turning over data from their Full Self-Driving (FSD) traffic violations. This is not just a paperwork snafu; it’s a legal tug-of-war. Regulators and safety advocates are eager to see this data to ensure that self-driving tech is actually safe on the roads.
For Tesla, it’s another day, another challenge. The company has always been at the forefront of innovation, but this situation highlights the ongoing debate about transparency and accountability in the autonomous vehicle space. If you're a Tesla owner, or just a fan of self-driving tech, this is definitely one to keep an eye on.
**NY SPORTS SEGMENT**
And now, let’s take a quick skate over to sports. Excitement is buzzing in New York as Jack Hughes, fresh off his Olympic gold medal win, is set to return to the Devils this Wednesday. Want to catch him in action? Well, it might cost you a pretty penny. Ticket prices are soaring, reflecting the hype and Hughes’ star power. But if you’re a die-hard fan, seeing him back on the ice might just be worth the splurge.
**OUTRO**
Alright, folks, that’s a wrap for today’s MikeCast. Thanks for tuning in and letting me be your news buddy. If you enjoyed today’s episode, don’t forget to subscribe and share it with a friend. Got thoughts or stories you want me to cover? Shoot me a message. Until next time, stay curious and keep asking questions. This is Mike, signing off. Catch you later!
item.itunes:title: MikeCast — February 23, 2026
